musicalLanguage.description: Henry Mancini's The Pink Panther Theme opens with the left hand entirely alone: the right hand is silent for the first 10 measures — the longest rest gap in the piece — so the famous bass ostinato has to establish the groove by itself before the melody enters at measure 11. Harmonically, the content-based key detection is essentially inconclusive here (margin just 0.01, well below a usable signal), so the stated E minor (statedKeyFit 0.351) remains the working key, but every one of the 24 chord windows that clears the confidence gate is rooted on F# — F# major (13 windows), F# dominant seventh (7 windows), and F# major seventh (4 windows) — at generally modest confidence (mostly between 0.155 and 0.528). None of these windows needed the harness's inferred-harmony hedge (this piece's texture is struck block chords, not arpeggios), but the confidence values themselves are honest about real ambiguity — this reads as a genuinely chromatic, riff-driven harmonic language rather than a clean functional progression. Structurally the piece is remarkably cyclical: a single 59-note right-hand passage covers 78.7% of all right-hand events across two long occurrences (measures 11-40 and 51-80), and a 61-note left-hand bass figure recurs four times on an exact 20-measure cycle (measures 20-29, 40-49, 60-69, and 80-89).
musicalLanguage.structure: A 10-measure left-hand-only introduction (measures 1-10) establishes the bass ostinato before the melody enters at measure 11, opening a long first statement that runs to measure 40. A second, nearly identical statement follows at measures 51-80 (the same 59-note right-hand passage, 78.7% coverage of all right-hand events), with the left hand's 20-measure bass cycle repeating underneath at measures 20-29, 40-49, 60-69, and 80-89. The section lens's two sharpest pivots, at measures 29 (novelty 8.114) and 69 (novelty 7.841), mark real structural turns inside those two long statements, and the piece closes with 2 measures of complete silence at measures 90-91.
keyMoments: The right hand is completely silent for the piece's first 10 measures — the longest rest gap anywhere in the piece — so the opening groove belongs entirely to the left hand's bass ostinato before the melody ever enters at measure 11.

Every labeled chord window in the piece is rooted on F#, cycling between F# major (13 windows), F# dominant seventh (7 windows), and F# major seventh (4 windows) at generally modest confidence (0.155 to 0.528) — this single-root, shifting-quality pattern is consistent with a chromatic riff rather than a functional chord progression.

A single 59-note right-hand passage accounts for 78.7% of all right-hand events in the piece, appearing complete at measures 11-40 and again at measures 51-80 — learning this one passage secures the overwhelming majority of the melodic material in the entire arrangement.

The left-hand bass figure repeats on an exact 20-measure cycle four separate times (measures 20-29, 40-49, 60-69, and 80-89) — recognizing this regular period turns what looks like 91 measures of new material into one bass line, restated on a metronomic schedule.
teachingGoals: Practice the 10-measure left-hand-only introduction until the groove is completely internalized — the entire character of the piece depends on this bass ostinato before any melody arrives.

Learn the single 59-note right-hand passage that covers 78.7% of the piece's right-hand content — since it appears at measures 11-40 and 51-80, mastering it once handles most of the melodic material outright.
Recognize the 20-measure left-hand cycle (measures 20-29, 40-49, 60-69, 80-89) as one repeating unit rather than four separate passages to learn independently.

Understand the F#-rooted, quality-shifting chord windows (major, dominant seventh, major seventh) as a chromatic riff language — listen for the shifting color rather than expecting a functional cadence to resolve it.
styleTips: Play the opening 10 measures with a locked, unwavering groove — since there's no melody yet to carry attention, rhythmic precision in the left hand alone has to hold the listener.

Pedal lightly, if at all, through the dense block chords built on F# — with confidence values this modest in the harness's own chord analysis, avoid using heavy pedal to imply a harmonic certainty the data doesn't support.

Listen for the shifting quality on the F# root — major, then dominant seventh, then major seventh — as the source of this piece's sly, chromatic character, and let your touch highlight that color shift rather than smoothing it over.

Try landing the final two measures of complete silence (90-91) as a clean, deliberate stop — after 89 measures built largely from two repeating cycles, the ending should read as a sudden full stop, not a fade.
